meta.name: Smoke Inhalation without Burns — CO Exposure

patient.name: Alicia Reyes
patient.chiefComplaint: Cough, chest tightness, dyspnea after firefighting interior operation

history.hpi: 45-year-old female firefighter pulled out of an interior attack by her RIT team when her SCBA alarm ran low during an aggressive interior operation. She was inside for approximately 20 minutes. No burns and no direct thermal injury, but she has a persistent cough, chest tightness, and progressive shortness of breath since removing her mask.
history.events: Interior attack operation. SCBA low-air alarm activated. RIT extraction. Removed mask outside. Symptoms developed over 10-15 minutes since extraction.

scene.dispatch: Medic 7, respond to active fire scene at 420 Industrial Way for firefighter rehab, one member with respiratory symptoms after interior operation.
scene.location: Warehouse fire scene, rehab sector set up 100 feet from the structure. Firefighter seated in rehab chair, still in bunker pants.
scene.safety: Rehab sector clear of smoke, upwind from the structure. Fire still active.
scene.bystanders: Other firefighters and safety officer present; incident commander coordinating
visualCues: Middle-aged female in firefighter uniform, seated in rehab
Soot on face, neck, and bunker gear
No visible burns or singed hair
Persistent cough that brings up gray-black sputum
Talking in short phrases, voice slightly hoarse
Pulse oximeter on the forefinger reads 99% — potentially misleading
Firefighter appears more ill than the SpO2 suggests

phases.name: Arrival — Post-Fire Exposure Respiratory Symptoms
phases.description: Firefighter with smoke inhalation and likely CO exposure. Classic clinical finding: patient LOOKS sick but SpO2 is normal. Pulse oximeter cannot distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin. Treat presumptively for CO with 100% oxygen via NRB. Also consider cyanide in severe cases with hemodynamic/neurologic compromise.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent; voice slightly hoarse but audible
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Mildly tachypneic at 22/min; mild wheezing bilaterally; productive cough with carbonaceous sputum
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Tachycardic from exertion and possible CO

skin.color: NOT cherry red (this is a myth); mild pallor

otherFindings: Soot on face and in nostrils
Headache, nausea, feeling 'off' — classic CO symptoms
SpO2 reads 99% — FALSELY REASSURING in CO exposure
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'I feel terrible... bad headache... chest feels tight'

expectedActions.action: Remove bunker gear and move to rehab sector; continue assessment in a clean-air environment
Apply 100% oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 15 LPM; treat presumptively for CO regardless of SpO2 reading
expectedActions.rationale: CO has an affinity for hemoglobin ~240x greater than oxygen. High FiO2 shortens the half-life of carboxyhemoglobin from 4 hours (room air) to ~80 minutes (100% O2).

expectedActions.action: Use CO-oximeter (SpCO) if available for quantitative CO exposure — 28% is moderate-severe; note that SpO2 standard pulse oximetry is unreliable
Assess airway for thermal injury signs (hoarseness, stridor, facial burns, singed hair, carbonaceous sputum) — monitor for progression to airway edema over the next hours
Obtain 12-lead EKG — CO can precipitate ischemia at lower thresholds
Consider cyanide toxicity if hemodynamic or neurologic compromise develops in an enclosed-space fire exposure; Hydroxocobalamin 5 g IV over 15 minutes if available and indicated
Administer Albuterol 5 mg + Ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized if wheezing from chemical bronchitis
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography

phases.name: Transport Decision — Hyperbaric Candidacy
phases.description: Patient is stable for transport but has moderate CO exposure (SpCO 28%). Consider hyperbaric oxygen facility for definitive treatment. Criteria typically include SpCO >25%, any neurologic symptoms, any cardiac symptoms, pregnancy, or loss of consciousness at scene.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Decreased cough and wheezing with oxygen and nebulizer

expectedActions.action: Continue 100% O2 via NRB throughout transport
Consider hyperbaric oxygen facility for definitive treatment if feasible — SpCO >25%, neurologic symptoms, cardiac changes, or loss of consciousness support HBO therapy
Monitor for delayed airway edema — voice change, stridor progression — over the transport window
Transport to hyperbaric-capable center if within reasonable time; otherwise to nearest ED
Notify receiving hospital of CO exposure, SpCO level, and symptoms

phases.name: Branch: Delayed Airway Edema
phases.description: During transport the patient's voice becomes progressively hoarser, mild stridor develops. Delayed airway edema from smoke inhalation can present 4-24 hours later. Crew must recognize the trajectory and escalate airway management.
phases.triggerCondition: Progressive voice change or stridor during transport

otherFindings: Delayed airway edema developing; time to reassess intubation decision

expectedActions.action: Recognize developing airway edema and discuss preemptive intubation with online medical control if transport time is long
Consider nebulized Epinephrine 5 mg (5 mL of 1 mg/mL) for supraglottic edema as a temporizing measure
Upgrade ED notification — patient may need ED-based airway management on arrival

criticalActions: Apply 100% oxygen via NRB regardless of SpO2 reading
Recognize that SpO2 is unreliable in CO exposure
Assess airway for signs of thermal injury and monitor for delayed edema
Consider cyanide toxicity in enclosed-space fires with hemodynamic or neurologic compromise
Transport to hyperbaric-capable facility if SpCO >25% or neurologic symptoms
expectedActions: Remove bunker gear for clean-air assessment
Document SpCO and symptoms for hyperbaric decision-making
bonusActions: Articulate why SpO2 is unreliable in CO

learningObjectives: Treat CO exposure with 100% oxygen regardless of SpO2
Understand the limitations of standard pulse oximetry in CO exposure
Consider cyanide toxicity in enclosed-space fires
discussionQuestions: Why does SpO2 read normal in CO exposure?
What are the criteria for hyperbaric oxygen therapy in CO poisoning?
When should you consider cyanide toxicity and how is it treated?
How long after a smoke inhalation exposure can airway edema develop?
Why don't CO patients typically present with 'cherry red' skin?
commonPitfalls: Using nasal cannula instead of NRB for CO exposure
Transporting to a non-hyperbaric facility when a nearby option exists
Failing to monitor for delayed airway edema after arrival at the hospital
Missing the option to treat cyanide toxicity
keyTakeaways: 100% O2 via NRB for all suspected CO exposure — SpO2 lies
Delayed airway edema is a real risk — watch voice and stridor
Hyperbaric is definitive for moderate-severe CO
